segments.title: HMS Victoria's forward guns, collision, and Jellicoe connection
segments.summary: HMS Victoria links an early all-forward heavy armament, the fatal 1893 Mediterranean Fleet collision, and John Jellicoe's later role in the Nelson-class design story.
segments.body: Victoria concentrated her 16.25-inch guns forward, the largest-calibre weapons yet fitted to a British battleship. Together with Sans Pareil, she shows that the Royal Navy had explored an all-forward heavy battery decades before Nelson and Rodney. Victoria was launched in 1887, commissioned in 1890, and grounded in 1892, but poor handling was not what destroyed her. In 1893 Admiral George Tryon ordered his Mediterranean Fleet squadrons to converge as a test of whether subordinate commanders would think for themselves; their compliance produced the fatal collision that cost Tryon his ship and his life. John Jellicoe survived Victoria's loss, later commanded the Grand Fleet in the First World War, and became instrumental in the creation of the Nelson-class battleships, completing the video's link between two generations of all-forward British capital ships.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Victoria's forward heavy guns, commissioning and grounding, Tryon's fatal convergence order, Jellicoe's survival, and the later Nelson and Rodney connection.
